title: Service Discovery & API Gateway
learningObjectives: Explain **service discovery** (registry, DNS, Kubernetes Endpoints) and why **stale routing** causes post-deploy 502s
Contrast **client-side** vs **server-side** discovery and where an **API gateway** adds **TLS, auth, and rate limits**
Map **Spring Cloud Gateway**, **Spring Cloud LoadBalancer**, and **Kubernetes Service** DNS to a concrete request path
Diagnose **readiness vs liveness** mistakes and **empty Endpoints** using **kubectl** and **gateway upstream** metrics
Describe **mTLS**, **JWT validation at the edge**, and **partner-facing** APIs without leaking **internal Service** DNS
Choose discovery patterns for **greenfield K8s** vs **hybrid VM** estates and name one **measurable trade-off**

sections.title: Why Service Discovery & API Gateway matters
sections.content:
When discovery and gateways are mishandled, production looks like random **502 Bad Gateway** waves right after a **rolling deploy**, even though **`kubectl get pods`** shows green. The real failure is often **empty or stale Endpoints**, **readiness** that does not match real dependencies, or a **gateway** still pointing at **draining IPs** while DNS and caches lag. Customers see timeouts; on-call chases ghosts because each layer thinks someone else owns routing truth.

Interviewers are not testing whether you memorized **Eureka** class names. They want to hear **where the truth lives** for **which backends are safe to call**, how **health** gates membership in the pool, and how **edge policy** differs from **domain logic**. Strong answers name **symptoms** (spiky 502, regional-only failures) and **signals** (**Endpoints** YAML, **upstream_rq_5xx**, **CoreDNS** QPS).

A crisp four-step answer usually covers: (1) **stable edge** DNS or VIP versus **volatile** instance sets, (2) **discovery mechanism** (registry, **kube-proxy**, mesh **EDS**), (3) **gateway responsibilities** (**TLS**, **JWT**, **rate limit**, **routing**), (4) **failure handling** (**timeouts**, **retries** on safe reads, **circuit breaking**) with **one command** you would run first (`kubectl get endpoints`).

At scale, this topic is how **fifty teams** share a **platform contract**. Bad defaults multiply: one service bypasses the **gateway** and invents **mTLS** rules another team cannot audit; another publishes **pod IPs** to mobile clients and turns every deploy into a **cache TTL** incident. Good platforms make **golden paths** obvious and measure **SLO** per route.

Senior answers sound **operational**: you discuss **watch churn** on huge **EndpointSlices**, **connection pool** reuse across **pod churn**, **JWKS** cache TTL at the gateway, and **canary** promotion tied to **error budget** — not only class diagrams.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Service Discovery & API Gateway
sections.content:
### Day 52 — Service discovery and API gateway in production systems

This day connects **how callers find backends** with **how organizations enforce policy** at the edge of a microservice fleet.

---

### 1. Plain definition
**Service discovery** answers: *what addresses are valid right now for service S?* Implementations include **client-side registries** (Eureka, Consul), **DNS** (Kubernetes **Service** names resolved by **CoreDNS**), and **mesh control planes** that program **Envoy** with **endpoint sets**. An **API gateway** is an **L7 reverse proxy** that terminates **TLS**, authenticates **clients**, applies **rate limits**, and **routes** paths to internal **Services** or **clusters**.

---

### 2. Mechanism — Kubernetes Service to pod IP
**CoreDNS** returns the **ClusterIP** (or **pod A records** for **headless** Services). **kube-proxy** or the **CNI** dataplane programs rules so traffic to the **VIP** fans out to **Endpoint** IPs that passed **readiness**. **Java** code using `http://payments:8080` relies on that chain. **Interview angle:** if **readiness** is wrong, **Endpoints** lie while pods look **Running**.

---

### 3. Client-side vs server-side discovery
| Style | Who picks the instance? | Pros | Cons |
|-------|-------------------------|------|------|
| Client-side | Service + library (**LoadBalancer**) | Flexible algorithms, fewer hops | Library sprawl, harder audits |
| Server-side | **LB** / **gateway** | Simple clients, central policy | Extra latency, must scale the middle |

**Interview angle:** on **Kubernetes**, **server-side** via **Service** is default; **client-side** appears with **Spring Cloud** or **mesh** sidecars.

---

### 4. API gateway responsibilities (and what does not belong)
Gateways should own **edge concerns**: **TLS**, **OAuth2/JWT** validation, **WAF** hooks, **per-client quotas**, **path routing**, **request size limits**, and **trace header** injection. They should **not** become a **business rules** monolith joining ten domains per request.

---

### 5. Most-probed interview angles
1. **502 after deploy** → **Endpoints**, **readiness**, **gateway upstream pool**, **stale connection** reuse.
2. **Eureka self-preservation** vs **Kubernetes** partition behavior — **stale registrations** trade-offs.
3. **mTLS** between gateway and services vs **JWT** for **end-user** identity — both may be required.
4. **Global vs regional** discovery — **latency**, **failover**, **data residency**.

**Interview angle:** always mention **timeouts** when you mention **retries** — blind retries amplify incidents.

---

### 6. Java / Spring mapping
- **`spring.cloud.gateway`** routes with **predicates** (`Path=/api/payments/**`) and **filters** (`AddRequestHeader`, **rate limit**).
- **`@LoadBalanced WebClient`** resolves **`http://payments`** via **Spring Cloud LoadBalancer** + **DiscoveryClient**.
- **`spring.cloud.kubernetes`** discovers **Endpoints** without Eureka when running **in-cluster**.
- **Actuator** **`/actuator/health/readiness`** should drive **Kubernetes** **readinessProbe**.

---

### 7. Comparison — Eureka vs pure Kubernetes
| Topic | Eureka (historical Java) | Kubernetes native |
|-------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Source of truth | Eureka server | **API server** **Endpoints** |
| Best fit | **Hybrid** VM + container | **Cluster-only** workloads |
| Ops cost | Extra cluster to run | **Platform-managed** |

Teams still on **Spring Cloud Netflix** should know **replacement** paths (**Kubernetes**, **Consul**, **mesh**).

---

### 8. Sixty-second story
**Symptom:** checkout **502** only from **mobile** after **payments** rollout. **Root cause:** **readiness** returned **UP** before **DB migration** finished; **Endpoints** advertised pods that returned **500**; **gateway** retried and amplified load. **Fix:** **readiness** checks **DB version** gate; **maxUnavailable** tuning; **retry** only on **idempotent** GET with **budget**. **Verify:** `kubectl get endpoints payments -o yaml` and **gateway** **upstream** metrics during canary.

---

### 9. Observability hooks
Watch **gateway** **5xx by route**, **upstream connect errors**, **DNS** latency from **CoreDNS** metrics, and **JVM** **pool** metrics on **gateway** pods during **large** **scaling** events.

sections.title: Basic — Discovery styles & gateway duties (reference tables)
sections.output:
=== How callers learn addresses ===
Mechanism              | Plain idea
DNS (kube Service)     | CoreDNS -> ClusterIP -> kube-proxy rules
Client registry        | App asks Eureka/Consul for instances
Mesh EDS               | Control plane pushes endpoints to Envoy sidecar
Static config file     | Breaks elastic scale; only bootstrap/dev

=== API gateway vs plain load balancer ===
Layer | Typical box        | Understands HTTP path / JWT?
L4    | NLB / TCP LB       | No — TCP streams only
L7    | API gateway        | Yes — routes, filters, auth at edge

=== Gateway duties (good vs bad) ===
Good at edge           | TLS, OAuth2/JWT check, rate limits, routing
Bad inside gateway     | Business pricing rules for every SKU

=== kubectl checks when routes fail ===
1. kubectl get endpoints <svc> -n <ns> -o wide
2. kubectl describe pod <pod> | find readiness
3. kubectl logs deploy/<gateway> --tail=200

=== Health probe meaning ===
readiness | Remove pod from Service when not safe for traffic
liveness  | Restart pod when process wedged — keep dependency checks here minimal
sections.description: Prints static interview tables: discovery mechanisms, L4 vs L7, gateway responsibilities, kubectl checks, readiness vs liveness.

=== Discovery + gateway simulation ===

--- Scenario 1: happy path — round-robin across orders pool ---
Route /api/orders -> 10.1.1.1:8080
Route /api/orders -> 10.1.1.2:8080
Route /api/orders -> 10.1.1.3:8080

--- Scenario 2: centralized policy at gateway ---
403 DENY missing credential (gateway policy)
ALLOW /api/orders

--- Scenario 3: discovery returns empty healthy set ---
502 no healthy upstream for payments

--- Scenario 4: summary rules ---
Rule 1: Gateways enforce edge auth, TLS, coarse rate limits.
Rule 2: Discovery lists must respect readiness—only healthy instances.
Rule 3: Clients need timeouts; discovery alone does not fix slow upstreams.
Rule 4: Prefer stable DNS at the edge; do not expose raw pod IPs to apps.

sections.title: Advanced — Route risk scoring + deployment pattern picks + decision table
sections.output:
=== Block 1: route complexity score ===
Higher priority => review gateway filters, timeouts, and canary first.

/api/checkout priority=27
/api/admin priority=15
/api/catalog priority=6

=== Block 2: discovery pattern cheat sheet ===
Pure Kubernetes microservices: Service DNS + Ingress or Gateway API.
Spring Cloud on VMs + K8s mix: Consul or Eureka until full migration.
Strict mTLS everywhere: mesh data plane + gateway at trust boundary.
Partner HTTP APIs: public gateway + JWT + WAF, never raw ClusterIP DNS.

=== Block 3: gateway / discovery decision reference ===
Symptom                               | First move
502 spike after rollout               | kubectl get endpoints + readiness
Clients hit stale pods                | shorten LB cache or recycle pools
Gateway CPU low but p99 high          | TLS/JWT filter or upstream latency
DNS storms                            | raise TTL carefully or cache in client
Cross-cluster routing                 | multi-cluster gateway or mesh federation
Unknown caller abuse                  | OAuth2 client credentials + per-key quota
Need path-based canary                | weighted route or Flagger + metrics
Spring upgrade breaks discovery       | align Spring Cloud BOM with Boot
Huge Service cardinality              | endpoint subsetting / split services
Debugging misses trace                | inject traceparent at gateway filter

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Using **liveness** probes for dependency checks so slow databases restart every pod in a loop while **readiness** should have removed them from traffic.
Publishing **pod IPs** or internal **ClusterIP DNS** to external partners, bypassing **auth**, **audit**, and **WAF** you would have enforced at a **gateway**.
Treating **DNS TTL** like a real-time registry so mobile clients cache **dead** backends for minutes after a deploy.
Running **gateway filters** that block the **reactive event loop** with synchronous JDBC calls, exploding **p99** under load.
Enabling **blind retries** from the gateway on **POST** without **idempotency keys**, doubling writes during upstream timeouts.
Letting **Spring Cloud LoadBalancer** cache stale instances forever after **TTL** misconfiguration while **Kubernetes** already moved **Endpoints**.
Putting **domain pricing rules** and **joins across ten services** inside the **gateway**, creating a **distributed monolith** front door.
Skipping **Endpoint** verification during incidents and only staring at **pod Running**, missing **empty subsets** that cause **502**.

sections.title: Exercise — Routing, discovery, and centralized policies (Day 52 assignment)
sections.problem:
Align this exercise with **Day 52 Assignment**: **Routing, discovery, and centralized policies**.

1. Build an in-memory **registry** that maps **logical service id** (`payments`, `orders`) to **instances** (`host:port` + **healthy** flag).
2. Build a **path routing table**: `/api/payments` → `payments`, `/api/orders` → `orders` (prefix match).
3. Implement a **gateway policy**: requests without a non-blank **API key** string must be rejected before routing (print a clear `403` style message).
4. For a matching route, print `ROUTE <path> -> <service> -> <chosen host:port>` choosing the **first healthy** instance, or a clear error if none.
5. Demonstrate in `main()` at least: **happy payment route**, **policy rejection**, **orders before registration** (error), then **register healthy orders** and show success.

Use only deterministic strings; no randomness.
hints: Keep route matching as simple prefix checks in a `LinkedHashMap` iteration order.
Separate **policy** from **discovery** so you can test each gate independently.
Register two **payments** instances with one unhealthy to prove **first healthy** selection.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is **service discovery** in microservices, and what breaks when it lies?
conceptual.answer: **Service discovery** answers *which backend addresses are valid right now* for a logical service name. **Kubernetes** implements this with **Service** objects, **CoreDNS** names, and **Endpoints** built only from **Ready** pods. When discovery is **stale** or **empty**, gateways and clients still open connections to **drained** or **broken** instances, so you see **502** spikes that line up with **rollouts** instead of steady load.

followUps.question: Name the first **kubectl** commands you run when checkout returns **502** only after a deploy.
followUps.answer: Start with **Endpoints truth**, not pod cosmetics: `kubectl get endpoints payments -n prod -o yaml` shows whether any **subsets** carry **ready** addresses. Pair with `kubectl get pods -l app=payments -o wide` and `kubectl describe pod` on failing pods to read **readiness** events. If you use **EndpointSlices**, run `kubectl get endpointslices -n prod --field-selector kubernetes.io/service.name=payments` to catch **controller** lag. Only after the cluster view is sane do you trust **gateway upstream** dashboards; otherwise you chase **ghost IPs** cached at the edge.

followUps.question: How does **Eureka self-preservation** differ from **Kubernetes** when the control plane is partitioned?
followUps.answer: **Eureka** may refuse to evict instances when it thinks too many heartbeats disappeared at once, which keeps **stale registrations** visible to avoid a thundering **empty registry** during partial outages. **Kubernetes** still drives **Endpoints** from **API server** state and **kubelet** readiness, so behavior depends on **node** and **apiserver** availability rather than client renewals. In interviews, explain the **trade-off**: Eureka protects against mass eviction noise but can prolong **bad routes**; Kubernetes is authoritative but can leave you with **no backends** if nodes are **NotReady**. Mention you would watch **SLO** and **error budget** before tuning eviction aggressiveness.

conceptual.question: Walk through **Kubernetes Service DNS** from a Java pod to a **Ready** upstream.
conceptual.answer: A pod resolves `payments.prod.svc.cluster.local` via **CoreDNS**, receiving the **ClusterIP** for the **payments** **Service**. **kube-proxy** or the **CNI** dataplane maps that **VIP** to **pod IPs** listed in **Endpoints** that passed **readiness**. Your **Java** process then opens a TCP connection to that **ClusterIP:port**, and the dataplane picks a **healthy** backend. If **readiness** is wrong, **DNS** still resolves but traffic lands on **bad** pods.

followUps.question: When would you choose a **headless** `clusterIP: None` Service instead of a normal **ClusterIP**?
followUps.answer: **Headless** Services return **pod A records** directly so clients or **StatefulSets** can target **specific** instances or run **peer discovery** without a shared **VIP**. They are common for **databases**, **Kafka** brokers, or gRPC clients that maintain their own **load balancing**. Trade-off: you expose **pod churn** to callers unless clients refresh DNS aggressively, so pair with **short TTL** patterns or **client-side** watchers. For ordinary **stateless** HTTP microservices behind a **gateway**, a normal **ClusterIP** is usually simpler because **kube-proxy** keeps fan-out opaque.

followUps.question: What **CoreDNS** metric or cluster signal hints **DNS is hot** during an incident?
followUps.answer: Watch **CoreDNS** request rates and latency histograms such as **`coredns_dns_requests_total`** and **`coredns_dns_request_duration_seconds`** in **Prometheus**. Spikes often track **client retry storms** or **misconfigured ultra-low TTL** that forces constant lookups. Also check **NodeLocal DNSCache** saturation if enabled, because **conntrack** or **CPU** on cache daemons can bottleneck before **CoreDNS** itself. In interviews, tie DNS heat back to **retry without backoff** or **N+1** service meshes of tiny calls that each resolve names independently.

conceptual.question: Contrast **client-side** discovery with **server-side** discovery for HTTP microservices.
conceptual.answer: **Client-side** means the caller library (**Spring Cloud LoadBalancer**, **Ribbon** successors, mesh **sidecar**) chooses an instance from a **registry** or **Endpoint** list. **Server-side** means a **load balancer** or **API gateway** picks upstreams while clients only know a **stable** edge name. **Client-side** adds flexibility and can save a hop, but spreads **policy** and **TLS** concerns across many binaries. **Server-side** centralizes **auth** and **quotas** but adds **capacity planning** for the middle tier.

followUps.question: Where does **`@LoadBalanced WebClient`** fit in this split?
followUps.answer: `@LoadBalanced` tells **Spring Cloud** to intercept `http://payments` style URIs and resolve them through **LoadBalancerClient** backed by **DiscoveryClient** data. That is classic **client-side** selection from the JVM’s point of view, even if the data ultimately mirrors **Kubernetes Endpoints**. Interviewers often probe failure modes: **stale cache**, **wrong service id**, missing **`spring.cloud.loadbalancer`** on the classpath after upgrades, or **blocking** calls on the reactive chain. Mention **`ReactorLoadBalancerExchangeFilterFunction`** for **WebClient** in WebFlux stacks.

followUps.question: Why might a platform team **forbid** bypassing the **gateway** for public mobile traffic?
followUps.answer: Mobile clients are untrusted and long-lived; exposing **internal Service DNS** or **pod IPs** skips **WAF**, **JWT** validation, **per-app rate limits**, and **central audit** of **API keys**. A **gateway** gives a **single place** to enforce **TLS versions**, **mTLS** to mesh ingress, and **bot** protections. The trade-off is you must **scale** the gateway tier and keep its **filters** non-blocking. Senior answers mention **PCI** scope reduction and **DDoS** absorption at the edge.

conceptual.question: What responsibilities belong in an **API gateway** versus inside domain services?
conceptual.answer: Gateways should terminate **TLS**, validate **OAuth2/JWT**, apply **coarse rate limits**, **route** paths, inject **trace** headers, and sometimes **terminate** **mTLS** to the mesh. **Domain services** own **business invariants**, **transactions**, and **data ownership**. When gateways start encoding **pricing rules** or **multi-service sagas**, deploys become **coupled** and **blast radius** grows because every team shares one **release train** for the edge.

followUps.question: How do you keep **Spring Cloud Gateway** filters from blocking the event loop?
followUps.answer: Prefer **non-blocking** filter implementations and offload blocking **JDBC** or **LDAP** to dedicated **auth** services or **reactive** clients. If you must block, isolate those routes on a **separate gateway deployment** with a **thread pool** or use **Spring MVC** gateway variants—never mix heavy blocking filters into a **high-QPS** reactive gateway without isolation. Watch **reactor** **event-loop** stalled threads and **Netty** pending tasks metrics. Interview answer: measure **p99** under load after each new filter and cap **CPU** per instance with **HPA** backed by **custom metrics**.

followUps.question: Give an example of a **gateway policy** that should stay centralized.
followUps.answer: **Per-client API key quotas** and **OAuth2 scopes** checks belong at the edge so every service does not reimplement **throttling** differently. Example: reject anonymous calls with **403**, map **client_id** to **tenant**, and stamp **X-Tenant-Id** for downstream **Row-Level Security**. Centralization avoids **policy drift** but requires a **versioned** contract for headers and **integration tests** that fail if a team bypasses the gateway in **staging**. Senior answers name **ownership**: platform **SRE** for **mesh** and **certs**, **application** teams for **readiness** semantics, and **security** for **WAF** rules—then explain how those teams coordinate during **sev-1** calls without blame loops.

conceptual.question: Explain **readiness** versus **liveness** in the context of **Service Endpoints**.
conceptual.answer: **Readiness** decides whether a pod receives **Service** traffic. Failing readiness removes the pod from **Endpoints**, which is what **kube-proxy** uses for routing. **Liveness** decides whether **kubelet** should **restart** the container. Putting **expensive dependency checks** on **liveness** causes **restart flapping**, while putting **no dependency checks** on **readiness** advertises pods that **500** real requests.

followUps.question: What does a **readiness** probe that only hits `/actuator/health` miss?
followUps.answer: The default **liveness** group may report **UP** while **readiness** groups for **database** or **Kafka** are still **DOWN**—if you accidentally probe the wrong group, you admit traffic too early. Also, a static **200** from a servlet that does not touch **DataSource** can hide **connection pool** exhaustion until user traffic arrives. Fix by using **`/actuator/health/readiness`** with **readiness groups** configured, and add **synthetic checks** that prove **migrations** applied when schema versions gate **traffic**.

followUps.question: How do **startup probes** change discovery during slow **JVM** warm-up?
followUps.answer: **Startup probes** give long **initialDelay** windows without killing the container, while **readiness** stays **failed** so **Endpoints** exclude the pod until warm-up completes. This prevents **Kubernetes** from routing to half-started **Spring** contexts. Mention pairing **startup** with **minReadySeconds** on the **Deployment** so **old** pods are not torn down until **new** pods pass **readiness** consistently across the **rolling** strategy. In real incidents you anchor the story with **time-aligned** graphs: **deploy** markers, **Endpoint** subset size, **gateway** **5xx** by **routeId**, and **client** **retry** rates so leadership sees causality instead of opinions.

conceptual.question: Why do teams see **502 from the gateway** right after a **rolling update**?
conceptual.answer: Common pattern: **new** pods are **Running** but not yet **Ready**, while **old** pods were already **terminated**, yielding **empty** or **undersized** **Endpoints** for a window. Another pattern: **gateways** or **Envoy** clusters cache **prior** upstream IPs until **health checks** fail. Less obvious: **maxUnavailable** set too aggressively drains **all** backends simultaneously.

followUps.question: Which **gateway metrics** differentiate **upstream reset** from **TLS handshake** failures?
followUps.answer: On **Envoy**, compare **`upstream_cx_connect_fail`** and **`upstream_rq_xx`** with **`tls_inspector`** stats; **Spring Cloud Gateway** exposes **Netty** channel metrics via **Micrometer** tags on **`spring.cloud.gateway.requests`** with **outcome** and **status**. **TLS** issues often spike during **certificate rotation** while **connect failures** track **Endpoint** churn. Mention capturing **downstream** **502** with **routeId** labels to know which **predicate** misbehaved. Senior answers name **ownership**: platform **SRE** for **mesh** and **certs**, **application** teams for **readiness** semantics, and **security** for **WAF** rules—then explain how those teams coordinate during **sev-1** calls without blame loops.

followUps.question: Name a **Deployment** field you tune to reduce **capacity gaps** during rollouts.
followUps.answer: **maxUnavailable** and **maxSurge** control how many **old** pods may disappear before **new** ones are **Ready**. Setting **maxUnavailable: 0** with **maxSurge: 1** is a classic pattern to avoid **zero** backends. Combine with **`minReadySeconds`** to catch **flapping readiness**. Interviewers like hearing you validate with **`kubectl rollout status`** and **HPA** headroom so **new** pods actually become **Ready** under load. Mention **verification** steps you would run in **staging** before promoting the same change to **prod**: **synthetic** **canaries**, **chaos** **drills** on **pod** **kill**, and **contract** tests that fail if a team bypasses the **gateway** accidentally.

conceptual.question: How does **Spring Cloud Gateway** route a request from **predicate** to **filter** to **URI**?
conceptual.answer: **RouteLocator** builds **Route** objects with ordered **GatewayFilter** chains and **AsyncPredicate** tests such as **Path**, **Host**, or **Header**. Incoming requests evaluate **predicates**; the first matching **route** wins unless you configure **ordered** routes explicitly. **Filters** can run **pre** and **post** logic—**AddRequestHeader**, **StripPrefix**, **RequestRateLimiter**, **OAuth2** resource server integration—and the **URI** may be `lb://payments` to integrate with **LoadBalancerClient**.

followUps.question: What breaks if **StripPrefix** is misconfigured with downstream services?
followUps.answer: Downstream **Spring MVC** controllers may expect `/api/payments` but receive `/payments` or vice versa, yielding **404** that look like **discovery** failures. Fix by aligning **`server.servlet.context-path`**, **gateway** **`filters: StripPrefix`**, and **OpenAPI** **servers** blocks. In interviews mention verifying with **`curl -v`** through the gateway in **staging** and **contract tests** that assert **paths** after rewrite. For **gateway** work, emphasize **non-blocking** **filters**, **connection** **pool** sizing to **upstream**, **TLS** session **resumption**, and **JWKS** **cache** **TTL** trade-offs that bite during **rotation** windows and **traffic** spikes.

followUps.question: How do you **rate limit** per **API key** at the gateway?
followUps.answer: Use **`RequestRateLimiter`** with a **Redis**-backed **`KeyResolver`** that reads a header such as **`X-Api-Key`** or a **JWT** claim. Configure **replenish rate** and **burst capacity** per tenant. Discuss **fail-open** versus **fail-closed** when **Redis** is down—product decision—and how to **alert** when limiter rejections spike. Mention **429** responses with **`Retry-After`** for friendly clients. Explain how you communicate to **non-engineers**: **customer** impact windows, **mitigation** in flight, **ETA** ranges, and **post-incident** **themes** that map to **roadmap** investments like **better** **readiness** or **multi-cluster** **failover**.

conceptual.question: What is the difference between **Ingress**, **Gateway API**, and a **Spring Cloud Gateway** deployment?
conceptual.answer: **Ingress** (Kubernetes) is a **north-south** HTTP/S routing resource implemented by controllers like **nginx** or **ALB**. **Gateway API** is the successor CRD model with richer **role-oriented** APIs. **Spring Cloud Gateway** is an **application-level** gateway you operate as **pods** with **Java** filters—often sitting **behind** cloud **L4/L7** load balancers. Teams combine **cloud LB** + **Ingress/Gateway API** + **Spring** gateway when they need **Java** ecosystem filters.

followUps.question: Where would you terminate **TLS** in that layered stack?
followUps.answer: Many clouds terminate **TLS** at the **managed LB**, re-encrypt to the **cluster** with **mTLS** or plain HTTP depending on threat model. **Spring Cloud Gateway** can terminate **TLS** again for **JWT** inspection or forward **mutual** certs. Interview answer balances **compliance** (**end-to-end** encryption) with **CPU** cost—**session resumption** and **hardware** acceleration matter at **high QPS**. Contrast **happy path** docs with **partition** behavior: **split brain**, **stale** **lists**, **degraded** **read-only** modes, and how **idempotency** keys prevent **duplicate** **writes** when **clients** **retry** during **routing** flaps.

followUps.question: When is **Gateway API** preferable to classic **Ingress** annotations?
followUps.answer: **Gateway API** offers **typed** routes, **reference grants**, and **multi-team** ownership with **GatewayClass** separation, reducing **annotation spaghetti** on **Ingress**. followUps.answer: **`upstream_health_check_failure`** and cluster **health** state stats help correlate **gateway** **502** with **bad** **pods**. Wire **Grafana** panels per **cluster** **name**. Explain how you communicate to **non-engineers**: **customer** impact windows, **mitigation** in flight, **ETA** ranges, and **post-incident** **themes** that map to **roadmap** investments like **better** **readiness** or **multi-cluster** **failover**. Reference **security** **baselines**: **mTLS** for **service** identity, **JWT** for **people**, **least privilege** **scopes**, **audit** logs at the **edge**, and why **PII** must never land in **URL** paths that **CDNs** or **access** logs retain.

seniorScenario.question: **Production:** After a **payments** deploy, **checkout** shows **30% 502** from the **API gateway** while **`kubectl get pods`** is all **Running**. Walk your response.
seniorScenario.answer:
**(1) Immediate response** Page **payments** and **platform** on-call, freeze **progressive** delivery if active, open **gateway** **502** rate by **routeId** and **payments** **golden signals** (success rate, latency). Announce status referencing **customer-visible** impact and **ETA** for first hypothesis.

**(2) Root cause** Most often **Endpoints** are **empty** or **partial** because **readiness** fails while containers stay **Running**, or **rolling** parameters briefly drain all **Ready** backends. Secondary: **gateway** **upstream** cache or **Envoy** **EDS** lag keeps **drained** **IPs**.

**(3) Fix** 1) `kubectl get endpoints payments -n prod -o yaml` and **`kubectl get endpointslices`**. 2) `kubectl describe pod` on newest pods for **probe** failures. 3) If strategy caused gap, patch **Deployment** **`maxUnavailable`/`maxSurge`** and **rollback** if needed. 4) Bounce **gateway** pods only after confirming **data plane** staleness. 5) Validate with **synthetic** **canary** through gateway before closing.

**(4) Prevention** Align **readiness** with **DB migration** gates, add **minReadySeconds**, run **pre-prod** **load** tests on **rollouts**, and alert on **Endpoints** **subset** size dropping below **2** during **business hours**.

followUps.question: How do you communicate **rollback** versus **forward fix** to stakeholders?
followUps.answer: Share **error budget** burn rate: if **SLO** is **critically** depleted, **rollback** restores **known** state within **minutes** while you **root-cause** offline. **Forward fix** is justified when **rollback** risks **data** **incompatibility** or **migration** **irreversibility**. Provide **timestamps** and **customer** **region** scope from **metrics** to avoid vague updates. Call out **second-order** effects: **retry** storms after **timeouts**, **thundering herds** when **caches** expire together, **cost** of **extra** **hops**, and **SLO** **risk** when **edge** **dependencies** (**Redis** for **rate limits**) become **single points** of failure.

followUps.question: Which **SLO** would you add specifically for **gateway→payments**?
followUps.answer: Track **availability** on **`routeId=checkout_payments`** with **99.9%** monthly target and **latency** **p99** under **budget**. **Burn-rate** alerts on **5xx** share from **upstream** versus **client** errors separate **routing** bugs from **app** defects. Interviewers listen for **runbook** habits: which **dashboard** you open first, which **kubectl** **invocation** proves membership, and when you choose **rollback** versus **forward fix** based on **error budget** burn. Senior answers name **ownership**: platform **SRE** for **mesh** and **certs**, **application** teams for **readiness** semantics, and **security** for **WAF** rules—then explain how those teams coordinate during **sev-1** calls without blame loops.

seniorScenario.question: **Design review:** A partner wants the **mobile** app to call **`http://payments.prod.svc.cluster.local`** directly to “reduce latency.” Your response?
seniorScenario.answer:
**(1) Immediate response** Reject for **production**; offer **public** **gateway** **hostname** with **JWT** + **API key** and optional **HTTP/3** **edge** tuning for latency. Schedule a **design** workshop with **security** and **mobile** leads the same week so the partner hears a **unified** platform stance rather than ad-hoc debate.

**(2) Root cause** The request confuses **in-cluster** **DNS** with **Internet** **reachability** and skips **security** **controls** **WAF**, **audit**, **rotation**, and **DDoS** protection. **`svc.cluster.local`** is not routable from the **public** Internet, and even **VPN** clients should not depend on **volatile** **pod** naming. **Latency** claims rarely survive **tracing** once you separate **TLS**, **payload**, and **device** **radio** effects.

**(3) Fix** 1) Publish **stable** **api.example.com** via **cloud** **LB** to **gateway**. 2) Issue **mTLS** or **JWT** **per partner** with **scoped** **audiences**. 3) Document **SLO**, **rate limits**, and **deprecation** policy in **OpenAPI**. 4) Provide **staging** **curl** **examples** that mirror **production** **headers**. 5) Add **WAF** rules and **bot** protections at the **edge**. 6) Run **load** tests proving **gateway** overhead is not the dominant **p99** term.

**(4) Prevention** Add **architecture decision record** banning **ClusterIP** **leakage**, **CI** checks for **hardcoded** **internal** **DNS** in **mobile** repos, **quarterly** **penetration** tests on **public** **surface**, and **executive** **review** for any **exception** that bypasses **central** **policy**.

followUps.question: How do you measure whether **gateway** **latency** is actually the bottleneck?
followUps.answer: Compare **p50/p95/p99** **end-to-end** with **segments**: **DNS**, **TLS**, **gateway** processing, **upstream** **payments**. Use **distributed tracing** **spans** tagged **`net.peer.name`**. If **gateway** adds **<2ms** median, **latency** claims should focus on **payload** size or **device** **network** instead. Mention **verification** steps you would run in **staging** before promoting the same change to **prod**: **synthetic** **canaries**, **chaos** **drills** on **pod** **kill**, and **contract** tests that fail if a team bypasses the **gateway** accidentally.

followUps.question: What **contract** clauses belong in a **partner** **API** agreement?
followUps.answer: Include **rate limits**, **deprecation** policy, **SLA** credits, **incident** **notification** channels, **data** **residency**, and **forbidden** **uses** (scraping, **resale**). Legal language should reference **gateway** **keys** rotation responsibilities. Contrast **happy path** docs with **partition** behavior: **split brain**, **stale** **lists**, **degraded** **read-only** modes, and how **idempotency** keys prevent **duplicate** **writes** when **clients** **retry** during **routing** flaps. Close with **prevention** that is measurable: **SLO** alerts on **empty** **Endpoints**, **synthetic** **auth** checks after **issuer** maintenance, **lint** rules blocking **internal** **DNS** in **mobile** repos, and **quarterly** **game days** with **written** outcomes.

seniorScenario.question: **Migration:** You move **200** **Spring Boot** services from **Eureka** to **Kubernetes** native discovery in **six months**. Outline the plan.
seniorScenario.answer:
**(1) Immediate response** Stand up **staging** **clusters** with **`spring-cloud-kubernetes`**, **RBAC** templates, and **golden** **Helm** charts for **probes** and **Service** naming. Publish a **migration** **playbook** with **office hours** so teams know how **serviceId** strings map to **DNS** names and which **dashboards** validate **instance** lists.

**(2) Root cause** Risk is **dual-truth** registries and **inconsistent** **health** semantics causing **flapping** **routes** during **cutover**. **Eureka** **self-preservation** and **Kubernetes** **readiness** are not equivalent; **JVM** apps may register before **Endpoints** would consider them **Ready**, or the reverse if **probes** are mis-tuned.

**(3) Fix** 1) Inventory **serviceId** mapping to **Kubernetes** **Service** names. 2) Pilot **low-risk** **stateless** APIs with **shadow** traffic comparisons. 3) Run **parallel** discovery with **feature** **flag** to switch **`lb://`** resolution source per **namespace**. 4) Decommission **Eureka** **AZ** by **AZ** after **metrics** prove **parity** on **registration** count and **5xx**. 5) Train **teams** on **`kubectl`** **Endpoints** debugging and **Spring** **Actuator** **groups**. 6) Automate **rollback** of the **flag** if **SLO** **burn** spikes during **cutover** windows.

**(4) Prevention** **Lint** **bootstrap** YAML for forbidden **Eureka** properties post-cutover, **dashboard** **discovery** **lag**, **game** days for **registry** **outage**, and **quarterly** audits ensuring **no** **new** **Eureka** **dependencies** sneak in via **copy-paste** templates.

followUps.question: How do you **budget** engineering time across **200** services?
followUps.answer: Use **tiering**: **P0** **customer** flows first, **P1** internal **APIs**, **P2** batch. Automate **scaffolding** updates and **reject** **manual** **per-service** snowflakes unless **risk** review approves. Track **migration** **burn-down** chart weekly. Reference **security** **baselines**: **mTLS** for **service** identity, **JWT** for **people**, **least privilege** **scopes**, **audit** logs at the **edge**, and why **PII** must never land in **URL** paths that **CDNs** or **access** logs retain. In real incidents you anchor the story with **time-aligned** graphs: **deploy** markers, **Endpoint** subset size, **gateway** **5xx** by **routeId**, and **client** **retry** rates so leadership sees causality instead of opinions.

followUps.question: What **metric** proves you can **decommission Eureka**?
followUps.answer: Zero **production** **traffic** relying on **Eureka** **DNS** names, **Eureka** **dashboard** **instance** count at **zero** for **30** days, and **no** **alerts** from **Eureka** **server** **JVM**. Backup **snapshots** of **registry** data before **power-off**. In real incidents you anchor the story with **time-aligned** graphs: **deploy** markers, **Endpoint** subset size, **gateway** **5xx** by **routeId**, and **client** **retry** rates so leadership sees causality instead of opinions. Interviewers listen for **runbook** habits: which **dashboard** you open first, which **kubectl** **invocation** proves membership, and when you choose **rollback** versus **forward fix** based on **error budget** burn.

seniorScenario.question: **Incident:** **JWT** validation at the **gateway** suddenly returns **401** for all **tenants** after an **issuer** cert rotation. What do you do?
seniorScenario.answer:
**(1) Immediate response** Trigger **severity** **1**, open **status** page if **customer** **auth** blocked, and **rollback** **gateway** **deployment** if a **bad** **config** change shipped; otherwise coordinate with **identity** team. Preserve **verbose** **logs** with **redacted** tokens for **post-incident** review while **support** communicates **workarounds** such as **temporary** **maintenance** pages if needed.

**(2) Root cause** **JWKS** fetch **failures**, **clock** **skew**, wrong **`issuer` URI**, or **stale** **cache** after **key** rotation **kid** changes. **Firewall** egress rules or **SPIFFE** **proxy** misconfiguration can block **JWKS** pulls even when **tokens** are valid. **Multi-tenant** gateways may point at the wrong **issuer** per **route** after a **bad** **merge**.

**(3) Fix** 1) `curl -v https://issuer/.well-known/openid-configuration` from **gateway** pod. 2) Inspect **gateway** logs for **`JwtException`** **signatures** and **kid** values. 3) Tune **`cacheDuration`** or **force** **refresh** after **identity** confirms **key** promotion. 4) Verify **NTP** on **nodes** and **pod** **clocks**. 5) Deploy **hotfix** with **relaxed** **cache** only if **security** accepts risk and **overlap** keys exist. 6) Validate **JWKS** **signature** **algorithm** allowlists match **issuer** policy.

**(4) Prevention** **Canary** **gateway** **config** changes, **synthetic** **login** **checks** every **minute**, **runbook** tying **issuer** **maintenance** to **platform** **calendar**, and **automated** tests that **fetch** **JWKS** from **staging** **before** **prod** **promotion** completes.

followUps.question: Why is **rollback** sometimes **unsafe** for **JWT** changes?
followUps.answer: If **tokens** were already issued with **new** **keys**, **rolling** back **gateway** **trust** might reject **valid** **sessions**, **logging** users out **globally**. Coordinate with **identity** on **key** **overlap** periods and **support** **multiple** **JWK** **entries** until **TTL** expires. Senior answers name **ownership**: platform **SRE** for **mesh** and **certs**, **application** teams for **readiness** semantics, and **security** for **WAF** rules—then explain how those teams coordinate during **sev-1** calls without blame loops.

followUps.question: How do **customers** perceive this failure versus **upstream** **500**?
followUps.answer: **401** feels like “**logged** **out**” and triggers **password** **resets** and **support** **tickets** **spikes** even when **credentials** are fine. **Communicate** clearly that **re-login** may be required and avoid blaming **users**. Contrast **happy path** docs with **partition** behavior: **split brain**, **stale** **lists**, **degraded** **read-only** modes, and how **idempotency** keys prevent **duplicate** **writes** when **clients** **retry** during **routing** flaps. Close with **prevention** that is measurable: **SLO** alerts on **empty** **Endpoints**, **synthetic** **auth** checks after **issuer** maintenance, **lint** rules blocking **internal** **DNS** in **mobile** repos, and **quarterly** **game days** with **written** outcomes.

seniorScenario.question: **Scale:** **Gateway** **CPU** is fine but **p99** latency doubled after adding a **synchronous** **fraud** **check** filter. Explain and remediate.
seniorScenario.answer:
**(1) Immediate response** **Feature** **flag** off the **filter** for **non-fraud** **critical** paths or **raise** **replicas** temporarily while **isolating** **hot** **routes**. Capture **before/after** **p99** **histograms** tagged by **routeId** so **leadership** sees objective **impact** and approves **engineering** time for a proper **async** rewrite.

**(2) Root cause** **Blocking** **I/O** on **Netty** **event** **loop** threads serializes **requests**, inflating **queueing** **delay** even when **CPU** looks **low**. **Event-loop** starvation shows up as **growing** **pending** tasks while **GC** and **CPU** remain calm. **Synchronous** **HTTP** clients inside **GatewayFilter** are a classic **foot-gun** during **Black Friday** scale.

**(3) Fix** 1) Move **fraud** call to **async** **WebClient** with **bounded** **concurrency** and **timeouts**. 2) **Offload** to **sidecar** or **external** **policy** **service** with **dedicated** **pool**. 3) Split **gateway** **deployment** for **heavy** **routes** so **blast** **radius** shrinks. 4) Add **metrics** for **`reactor`** **event-loop** **pending** tasks and **Netty** **eventloop** **pending** **queues**. 5) Add **bulkhead** limits per **tenant** if **fraud** **provider** slows down.

**(4) Prevention** **Architecture** **review** **checklist** bans **blocking** **filters**, **load** **test** **gateway** with **k6** **per** **route**, **alert** on **scheduling** **delay** in **Micrometer**, and **profile** **gateway** **releases** on **hardware** similar to **prod** **nodes**.

followUps.question: How do you **prove** **event-loop** **blocking** in **profiling**?
followUps.answer: Capture **async-profiler** **wall** **clock** stacks on **gateway** pods during **incident** **replay**, look for **`jdbc`** or **`HttpURLConnection`** on **reactor-http** threads. **Arthas** **trace** can also show **hot** **methods**. In real incidents you anchor the story with **time-aligned** graphs: **deploy** markers, **Endpoint** subset size, **gateway** **5xx** by **routeId**, and **client** **retry** rates so leadership sees causality instead of opinions. Interviewers listen for **runbook** habits: which **dashboard** you open first, which **kubectl** **invocation** proves membership, and when you choose **rollback** versus **forward fix** based on **error budget** burn.

followUps.question: What **business** **trade-off** do you present when **async** adds **complexity**?
followUps.answer: **Async** **chains** need **clear** **timeout** and **fallback** policies to avoid **silent** **drops**. The **trade-off** is **engineering** **cost** versus **availability**; **executives** care about **fraud** **loss** versus **checkout** **conversion** **SLO**. Senior answers name **ownership**: platform **SRE** for **mesh** and **certs**, **application** teams for **readiness** semantics, and **security** for **WAF** rules—then explain how those teams coordinate during **sev-1** calls without blame loops. Mention **verification** steps you would run in **staging** before promoting the same change to **prod**: **synthetic** **canaries**, **chaos** **drills** on **pod** **kill**, and **contract** tests that fail if a team bypasses the **gateway** accidentally.

seniorScenario.question: **Chaos:** During an **AZ** **failure**, **discovery** shows **healthy** **pods** in **surviving** **AZ** but **clients** still **timeout**. Diagnose.
seniorScenario.answer:
**(1) Immediate response** Shift **traffic** at **global** **load** **balancer** to **healthy** **region**, drain **bad** **AZ** in **DNS** **weights**, and **collect** **packet** **traces** from **failing** **clients**. Engage **cloud** **network** **support** early if **BGP** or **transit** anomalies appear on **provider** **status** pages.

**(2) Root cause** **Subnet** **routing** **blackholes**, **stale** **security** **group** rules, **cross-AZ** **NAT** **exhaustion**, or **clients** **caching** **old** **gateway** **IPs**. **Discovery** can be **truthful** while **data** **plane** **paths** are broken, so **symptoms** look like **timeouts** rather than **502**. **Stateful** **connections** pinned to **bad** **AZ** **ENIs** exacerbate the pain.

**(3) Fix** 1) Verify **cloud** **health** **checks** for **NLB** **targets** and **target** **deregistration** delays. 2) **Revoke** **routes** advertising **dead** **AZ** and **confirm** **propagation** in **route** tables. 3) **Recycle** **connection** **pools** on **gateways** and **Envoy** **clusters** if **EDS** lag suspected. 4) **Lower** **TTL** temporarily with **ops** approval and **communicate** **client** **cache** risk. 5) **Failover** **databases** if **writes** **stuck** and **RPO** allows. 6) Validate **security** **groups** allow **surviving** **subnets** to reach **NAT** gateways.

**(4) Prevention** **Multi-AZ** **integration** tests, **quarterly** **AZ** **drain** drills, **SLO** on **regional** **failover** **minutes**, **dashboards** for **NAT** **port** **allocation**, and **documented** **RTO** **targets** per **tier** so **executives** know expected **customer** **impact** windows.

followUps.question: When do you **escalate** to **network** **team** versus **app** **team**?
followUps.answer: If **synthetic** **TCP** **connect** fails from **multiple** **services** to the **same** **VIP**, **network** owns **routing**. If **only** one **service** fails while **others** succeed, **app** **team** owns **misconfigured** **Service** **selectors** or **NetworkPolicy**. Close with **prevention** that is measurable: **SLO** alerts on **empty** **Endpoints**, **synthetic** **auth** checks after **issuer** maintenance, **lint** rules blocking **internal** **DNS** in **mobile** repos, and **quarterly** **game days** with **written** outcomes.

followUps.question: How do **SRE** **error** **budgets** change during **regional** **failover**?
followUps.answer: **Freeze** **feature** launches, **prioritize** **stability** work, and **communicate** **reduced** **SLO** targets until **steady** state returns. **Post-incident** review assigns **action** items with **deadlines** for **automation** gaps discovered. Explain how you communicate to **non-engineers**: **customer** impact windows, **mitigation** in flight, **ETA** ranges, and **post-incident** **themes** that map to **roadmap** investments like **better** **readiness** or **multi-cluster** **failover**. Reference **security** **baselines**: **mTLS** for **service** identity, **JWT** for **people**, **least privilege** **scopes**, **audit** logs at the **edge**, and why **PII** must never land in **URL** paths that **CDNs** or **access** logs retain.

wrongAnswers: **The API gateway should own all business rules** — **Correction:** Gateways excel at **TLS**, **auth**, **rate limits**, and **routing**. Pushing **domain joins** into the gateway creates a **distributed monolith** that is hard to test and deploy independently.

**Kubernetes DNS TTL alone keeps routing fresh during pod churn** — **Correction:** **TTL** is coarse; clients and resolvers **cache** aggressively. **Truth** for membership lives in **Endpoints** / **EndpointSlices** updated by the **API server**, not DNS record churn speed.

**If pods are Running, traffic must be healthy** — **Correction:** **Running** only means containers started. **Readiness** gates **Service** membership; misconfigured probes yield **empty Endpoints** while pods look fine, producing **502** at the **gateway**.

**Eureka and Kubernetes discovery are interchangeable with zero migration work** — **Correction:** **Eureka** is a **JVM-centric registry** with **self-preservation** semantics; **Kubernetes** uses **API server** watches. Clients, **health** models, and **partition** behavior differ; expect **config and library** upgrades.

**Client-side discovery always reduces latency versus a gateway** — **Correction:** You may save a hop, but you pay in **library coupling**, **key rotation**, and **policy** sprawl. Many teams prefer a **gateway** for **edge consistency** even if **p99** adds a millisecond.

**mTLS at the gateway removes the need for service-level security** — **Correction:** **Gateway mTLS** to upstreams is one hop. **Mesh mTLS** covers **east-west** between many services. You still need **authorization** and **data protection** inside services.

**Spring Cloud LoadBalancer caches forever for performance** — **Correction:** **Infinite TTL** yields **stale instances** after incidents. Tune **spring.cloud.loadbalancer.cache.ttl** and prefer sources that reflect **Kubernetes** **Endpoint** changes quickly.

**Global DNS geolocation alone implements active-active failover** — **Correction:** **DNS failover** is slow and **cache-dependent**. Real **active-active** pairs **health checks**, **traffic weights**, and often **multi-cluster gateways** or **mesh** with explicit **failover policy**.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Topic | Rule of thumb | Interview one-liner |
|---|---|---|
| Service discovery | Truth for live backends | Stale registry = post-deploy 502 |
| Kubernetes Service | VIP + Endpoints from readiness | describe pod vs get endpoints |
| API gateway | L7 edge policy | TLS + JWT + rate limit live here |
| Client-side LB | Library picks instance | Great flexibility, fleet-wide upgrades |
| Readiness | Gate traffic membership | Wrong probe = empty or bad pool |
| Eureka | JVM registry history | Know self-preservation + migration |
| Spring Cloud Gateway | Predicate + filter chain | Order matters; keep filters non-blocking |
| CoreDNS | In-cluster name resolution | Debug with nslookup from debug pod |
| mTLS | Service identity | Complements JWT for users |
| Canary | Weighted routes + metrics | Roll back weights, not hope |
